questions.prompt: Why model slippage as size-dependent rather than a fixed bps amount?

choices.text: Slippage scales with size relative to typical liquidity. Trading 100 shares of AAPL is essentially free (well within inside depth); trading 100,000 shares walks the book and costs significantly more per share. A fixed-bps model assumes the same friction regardless of size, hiding the most important fact: small sizes are cheap, large sizes are expensive. Real model: half-spread (constant) + size-dependent term (e.g., 1bp per 1% of daily volume) + cushion (5-10 bps for safety). Now backtests at $10K vs $1M produce different (and accurate) PnL — capacity becomes visible. Without this, you ship a strategy that worked at hobby scale and discover liquidity issues only when you scale up.

questions.explanation: Slippage scales with size — fixed bps misses capacity issues. Use half-spread + size-dependent term.

questions.prompt: Strategy backtests with Sharpe 1.5 at $10K capital; same Sharpe at $1M. Suspicious?

choices.text: Yes — real strategies degrade as capital grows because size affects slippage. If $1M trades the same names as $10K, the larger size eats more liquidity per trade and Sharpe should drop noticeably. Identical performance at 100× capital almost certainly means the cost model doesn't include size-dependent slippage — it's slipping by the same fixed bps at both scales, which is unphysical. Diagnose: switch to a size-dependent slippage model, re-run; Sharpe should fall at $1M and stay similar at $10K. If a strategy's real capacity is $100K and you ran it at $10M, live PnL will be far worse than backtest. Capacity testing should be part of every strategy validation.

questions.explanation: Identical Sharpe at 100× capital = cost model missing size-dependent slippage. Suspicious.
